sections.title: Purpose & Scope
sections.content:
This procedure describes the process for conducting drug and alcohol (D&A) testing on a FIFO mine site, including breath alcohol testing and urine drug screening, with full chain of custody documentation.

D&A testing on a mine site is a workplace safety function, not a law enforcement function. The site clinician's role is to conduct the test correctly, maintain chain of custody, and report results accurately. The consequences of a positive result (stand-down, investigation, referral to assistance programme) are managed by the employer per the site D&A policy — the clinician does not make employment decisions.

This procedure must be read in conjunction with the site-specific D&A policy. All testing must comply with Australian Standard AS/NZS 4308:2008 (Procedures for specimen collection and the detection and quantitation of drugs of abuse in urine) and AS 4760:2019 (Procedures for specimen collection and the detection and quantitation of drugs in oral fluid).

sections.title: Types of Testing
sections.content:
There are four types of D&A testing conducted on mine sites:

1. Pre-access — conducted before a worker enters site or returns from leave. Typically performed at the accommodation village, site gate, or clinic at the start of a swing. Workers who refuse cannot access site.
2. Random — unannounced testing of randomly selected workers during a shift. Workers are selected using a random number generator or equivalent random method — the clinician does not select who is tested. Workers must be notified of the test without prior warning. The clinician cannot be asked to selectively test or exclude specific individuals.
3. Post-incident — conducted after any significant safety incident, near miss, dangerous occurrence, or work-related injury. All workers involved or potentially involved in the incident should be tested as soon as practicable after rendering first aid and securing the scene. Time from incident to test must be documented — the longer the delay, the more defensible the result must be.
4. Reasonable cause — initiated when there is a reasonable, documented belief based on objective signs that a worker may be impaired. Observable signs must be documented in writing before testing begins (e.g., slurred speech, smell of alcohol, unsteady gait, erratic behaviour, falling asleep at the wheel). Reasonable cause testing initiated on the basis of one person's opinion without documented observation is high-risk — ensure a second observer is present if possible.

sections.title: Breath Alcohol Testing Process
sections.content:
Breath alcohol testing must be conducted with a calibrated, approved breathalyser in accordance with the manufacturer's instructions:

1. Confirm the breathalyser is within its calibration period — check the calibration sticker or certificate. Do not use an out-of-calibration device.
2. Instruct the worker not to eat, drink, smoke, or use oral spray for 15 minutes before testing. Document the time this instruction was given.
3. At testing time, insert a new, sealed mouthpiece into the device.
4. Instruct the worker to blow steadily and continuously into the device until the device signals completion.
5. Read and record the result immediately — do not wait. Record the reading in mg/100mL or g/210L as displayed by the device.
6. If the first reading is above the site threshold (typically 0.00 g/210L or 0.02 g/210L depending on site policy), conduct a second test after a minimum 15-minute wait. The 15-minute wait allows the mouth alcohol effect to dissipate.
7. Use the second reading as the result of record.
8. If the worker refuses to blow adequately (deliberately short blows, interrupting the sample), document the refusal. Refusal is treated the same as a positive result under most site policies — confirm this with your site D&A policy.
9. Complete the chain of custody form: device model and serial number, calibration date, test date and time, worker name and ID, first reading, second reading (if applicable), clinician name and signature, worker signature.

sections.title: Urine Drug Screen Process
sections.content:
Urine drug screening is a two-stage process: point-of-care immunoassay screen followed by laboratory confirmation for non-negative results.

Collection:
1. Verify the worker's identity (name, date of birth, employee ID).
2. Instruct the worker to remove outer clothing and leave all bags and personal items outside the collection area.
3. Provide the worker with a sealed, approved collection kit (cup with temperature strip and tamper-evident seal).
4. Direct observation of the void is not required unless there is a documented, specific reason to suspect adulteration (e.g., prior confirmed attempt to substitute). Document your reasoning if you elect direct observation.
5. Worker provides specimen in private. Specimen must be at least 30 mL.
6. Immediately upon receiving the specimen, check the temperature strip — acceptable range is 32–38°C. A specimen outside this range may indicate substitution or adulteration. Document the temperature.
7. Inspect the specimen for unusual colour, odour, or clarity. Document any abnormality.
8. If adulteration is suspected, document your observations, inform the worker, and handle per site policy (may include a witnessed re-collection).

Point-of-Care Screening:
1. Use an approved immunoassay screening device.
2. Process the specimen per the manufacturer's instructions.
3. Read the result at the correct time (typically 5 minutes — do not read early or late).
4. Record the result as: Negative (all lines present) or Non-Negative (one or more absent lines).
5. Never report a point-of-care screen result as "Positive" — the correct term is Non-Negative. Only GC-MS laboratory confirmation is reported as Positive.

Chain of Custody and Laboratory Submission:
1. In the presence of the worker, pour the specimen into the primary and split sample containers using the approved kit.
2. Apply tamper-evident seals in front of the worker.
3. Both the clinician and the worker sign across the seals and on the chain of custody form.
4. Package the specimen for laboratory transport per the chain of custody requirements.
5. For non-negative results: the primary sample is sent for GC-MS confirmatory testing. The split sample is retained for potential independent challenge testing.
6. Document collection date and time, worker name and ID, specimen ID number, seals applied, and signatures.

sections.title: Cutoff Levels
sections.content:
The following cutoff concentrations apply under AS/NZS 4308:2008 for urine drug screening:

- Cannabis (THC metabolite): Screening 50 ng/mL / Confirmatory 15 ng/mL
- Amphetamine-type stimulants (ATS): Screening 300 ng/mL / Confirmatory 150 ng/mL
- Opioids (morphine/codeine): Screening 300 ng/mL / Confirmatory 150 ng/mL
- Cocaine metabolites (benzoylecgonine): Screening 300 ng/mL / Confirmatory 150 ng/mL
- Benzodiazepines: Screening 200 ng/mL / Confirmatory 100 ng/mL
- MDMA: Screening 300 ng/mL / Confirmatory 150 ng/mL

Note: some mine sites apply lower cutoffs than the AS/NZS standard — refer to the site-specific D&A policy for the applicable thresholds.

A result below the screening cutoff is reported as Negative regardless of actual drug use — this is a feature of the cutoff system, not a limitation you should explain to the employer. A result above the screening cutoff but below the confirmatory cutoff on GC-MS is reported as Negative at confirmation.

sections.title: Stand-Down and Challenge Process
sections.content:
Following a non-negative urine screen or a positive breath alcohol result:

1. Inform the worker of the result clearly and without judgment.
2. Implement the stand-down per site policy — the worker is removed from safety-critical duties pending confirmation. This is an administrative action, not a disciplinary action at this stage.
3. Maintain confidentiality — do not discuss the result with other workers, supervisors beyond what is required by policy, or non-essential personnel.
4. Inform the worker of their rights:
- Right to contact the Employee Assistance Programme (EAP)
- Right to request a review or challenge of the result (split sample analysis at a laboratory of their choice at their cost)
- Right to provide information about legally prescribed medications that may have contributed to the result (this may affect interpretation at the confirmatory stage — the medical review officer process)
5. Do not make an employment decision — that is the employer's role. Your role is to accurately report the result and ensure the process was followed correctly.

sections.title: Documentation
sections.content:
Chain of custody documentation is mandatory for all samples sent for laboratory analysis. The chain of custody form must be fully completed and must accompany the specimen to the laboratory.

Retain copies of the following:
- Chain of custody form (clinician copy)
- Breathalyser printout or hand-written record with device serial number
- Incident report for post-incident testing
- Reasonable cause observation record for for-cause testing
- Laboratory result when received (file in worker's confidential medical record)

All D&A testing records are confidential health and employment records. Retain per site medical records policy. The employer is entitled to receive the result (Negative / Non-Negative / Confirmed Positive) and the chain of custody documentation — not the full clinical record.

sections.title: Regulatory Basis
sections.content:
- AS/NZS 4308:2008 — Procedures for specimen collection and the detection and quantitation of drugs of abuse in urine
- AS 4760:2019 — Procedures for specimen collection and the detection and quantitation of drugs in oral fluid
- Mines Safety and Inspection Regulations 1995 (WA) — drug and alcohol requirements
- Work Health and Safety Act 2020 (WA) — duty to manage impairment risk
- Privacy Act 1988 (Cth) — health information handling requirements
- Site-specific Drug and Alcohol Management Plan (DAMP)
